answer:
**In the Indic philosophical tradition as represented in the Falsafa corpus, *śruti* (revealed/heard) refers to the Vedas as direct, infallible divine revelation, while *smṛti* (remembered) denotes the traditional corpus of Dharmaśāstras and related texts authored by sages, which interpret, supplement, and apply *śruti*.**[^1]

*Śruti* signals absolute, unquestionable authority as the primordial "root-source" (*mūla*) of *dharma*, emanating directly from the divine (e.g., Prajāpati or the Self-existent One). It is the Veda itself, beyond human composition, and takes precedence in conflicts or gaps.[^2] By contrast, *smṛti* claims derivative authority as "sacred treatises on *dharma*" aligned with *śruti*, the conduct of the virtuous (*sadācāra*), and self-satisfaction (*ātmanuṣṭhi*), forming a "fourfold, direct evidence" (*pramāṇa*) of *dharma*. It is not to be "questioned in any matter" but is subordinate: a *twice-born* must follow *śruti* first, then *smṛti*, lest they be deemed "an atheist and a reviler of the Veda."[^3]

This hierarchy ensures *smṛti*'s legitimacy through fidelity to *śruti* (e.g., "whatever Dharma... is set forth in the Veda"), preventing rationalistic deviation while allowing practical extension.[^4] *Smṛti* thus signals mediated, tradition-bound authority—humanly recollected yet divinely sanctioned—differing from *śruti*'s unmediated revelation.

**The primary discussion appears in *Manusmṛti* Chapter 2 (verses 6–12), a foundational *smṛti* explicitly contrasting the two:**[^5]

- *Manusmṛti* 2.6–7 establishes *śruti* (Veda) and *smṛti* as co-sources with *sadācāra*.[^6]
- 2.8–10 equates *śruti* with Veda and *smṛti* with Dharma treatises, both unquestionable.[^7]
- 2.12 lists the "fourfold evidence": Veda/*śruti*, sacred tradition/*smṛti*, virtuous conduct, and self-satisfaction.[^8]

*Yājñavalkya Smṛti* 1.3 and 1.7 affirms Vedas/*śruti* and *smṛti* as "sources" (*pramāṇa*) of *dharma* alongside *sadācāra*, without the same granularity.[^9] No other *smṛti* in the corpus (e.g., *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, *Nārada Smṛti*) explicitly parses the distinction.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti* 2.10.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-4aaac7]

[^2]: *Manusmṛti* 2.6, 2.12–13.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d70b97] [https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-608b3f]

[^3]: *Manusmṛti* 2.9–11.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d16075]

[^4]: *Manusmṛti* 2.7.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-b75075]

[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 2 (full chapter overview).[/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/]

[^6]: *Manusmṛti* 2.6–7.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d70b97]

[^7]: *Manusmṛti* 2.8–10.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-76188a]

[^8]: *Manusmṛti* 2.12.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-608b3f]

[^9]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* 1.3, 1.7.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-b83e2e]
